perturbation.original_text: Baidu Net/Party A's Website means the Internet website owned by Party A whose domain name is http://www.baidu.com, through which Party A provides search services to its users.
perturbation.changed_text: Baidu Net/Party A's Website refers to a platform operated by Party A for providing online services.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifically defines Baidu Net/Party A's Website as the internet website with the domain name http://www.baidu.com. By removing the specific URL, and rephrasing it as 'a platform', we create ambiguity. Later in the contract, references to 'Baidu Net' assume it is clearly defined and maintained at a specific domain, particularly in the clauses relating to channel cooperation and domain names. Removing the URL creates an in-text contradiction in sections which assume a fixed location and identity for Baidu Net.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the term of this Agreement, Party B will use the Baidu's Real Estate and Home Furnishing Channel as the jointly formed channel of Party A and Party B. Party A grants all-round exclusive rights to Party B to construct, maintain and operate the Cooperation channel. As the owner of www.baidu.com, Party A has ownership and control over second tier domain names. Party A has the right to deprive Party B of the operating rights of the website without any liability if Party B is found in violation of any law. Party A is required to receive written consent from Party B prior to its adjustment of any second tier domain names which is under independent operation of Party B.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific domain name 'http://www.baidu.com' from the definition of 'Baidu Net/Party A's Website', rephrasing it as 'a platform'. The contradicted text refers to 'www.baidu.com' and 'second tier domain names' under Party A's ownership, implying a specific and identifiable website. By removing the URL in the changed text, the contract introduces ambiguity regarding the location and identity of 'Baidu Net'. This directly contradicts the section that assumes a defined and fixed location through the domain name, creating a strong in-text contradiction as the assumption is no longer valid due to the new definition.

perturbation.original_text: PRC Laws mean any laws, regulations, rules and regulatory documents in the PRC which are current and will be issued going forward.
perturbation.changed_text: PRC Laws mean regulations applicable to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the detailed definition and replacing it with "regulations applicable to this agreement," we remove the assurance that all relevant laws, rules, and regulatory documents (both current and future) will be considered. The term PRC Laws is used in warranties and representations. The removal creates an in-text contradiction as it creates uncertainty. Later references to 'PRC Laws' assume a broad and comprehensive understanding of these laws, and now that's ambiguos.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As of the date of this Agreement, it is bound by this Agreement. (b) It warrants that none of its execution, delivery and performance of this Agreement or conduct of any transaction contemplated hereunder is in violation of any PRC laws or any agreement to which it is a party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'PRC Laws' from 'any laws, regulations, rules and regulatory documents in the PRC which are current and will be issued going forward' to simply 'regulations applicable to this agreement.' This omission directly contradicts the warranty in the 'Contradicted Text' that the agreement does not violate 'any PRC laws,' because the warranty now lacks a clear and comprehensive scope. It creates uncertainty whether the warranty covers laws beyond just 'regulations applicable to this agreement', potentially exposing the warranting party to liability if other 'PRC Laws' (as originally defined) are violated. This aligns with the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' definition, which states that contradictions occur when essential language is removed, creating conflict with other sections.
